source.title: Transdermal scopolamine alters phasic REM activity in normal young adults.

confidence_rationale: Small (n=8) randomised crossover PSG study showing that transdermal scopolamine -- a clinically used muscarinic antagonist -- suppresses phasic REM activity and REM density at the lowest commercial dose. This is the substrate-level mechanism plausibility evidence for Q-031 pathway 1: anticholinergic drugs do measurably alter REM-related signal in healthy humans. But the study is small, young-adult, and short, so its transfer to elderly chronic-use anticholinergic patients is a real leap.
failure_signatures: n=8 healthy young men is a tiny sample with limited power; phasic REM measures (REM density, total REM activity) reach significance, but tonic REM measures (duration, latency) do not -- which complicates the simple 'scopolamine suppresses REM' reading.

The study is single-night, low-dose, healthy young adults. Q-031 cares about decades of nightly anticholinergic exposure in older adults with comorbidities -- the transfer from this acute experimental result to the chronic clinical context is large.

Scopolamine is not the most common chronic anticholinergic: most Q-031-relevant exposures are bladder antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, tolterodine) or tertiary tricyclics. CNS penetration profiles differ; the substrate evidence here is genus-level not species-level.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Transdermal scopolamine (1.5 mg patch) significantly suppresses phasic REM components -- total REM activity and REM density -- in healthy young adult men, even at the lowest commercial dose, while leaving tonic REM measures (REM duration, REM latency) unchanged.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-031 pathway 1 substrate evidence: confirms that a clinically relevant muscarinic antagonist measurably alters REM-related neural signature in humans. Useful as plausibility for the upstream half of the mediation chain (anticholinergic exposure --> REM disturbance) but not sufficient to establish that chronic bladder-antimuscarinic exposure produces the same kind or magnitude of REM disruption that would mediate dementia risk.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Acute single-night transdermal scopolamine in young healthy men is a substantial transfer step from the chronic, oral, often-tertiary-amine bladder antimuscarinics that drive Q-031's clinical relevance. The selective effect on phasic (not tonic) REM is also worth noting -- earlier injection studies of scopolamine showed much larger effects on REM latency and duration. Dose, route, and duration matter.
mapping.source_context: Randomised double-blind crossover, n=8 healthy young adult men, three nights including one adaptation, transdermal scopolamine 1.5 mg vs placebo patch, full PSG with phasic and tonic REM scoring.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality is moderate (well-designed crossover, blinded, full PSG, but n=8 is small and old data 1999). Mapping fidelity is moderate -- scopolamine is an anticholinergic, REM is the proposed mediator, the direction is right, but it is acute young-adult evidence rather than chronic-elderly. Transfer risk is high because the chronic bladder antimuscarinic / chronic tricyclic context Q-031 cares about is several steps from this acute scopolamine experiment.
